facts_disclosed: Raymond's Santa Fe Indian Market booth Aug 16, $4,200 concho belt

turns.content: yá'át'ééh. I need help thinking through logistics for something coming up this summer
Yá'át'ééh! I'd be happy to help with logistics. What's coming up this summer?
my husband got a booth at Santa Fe Indian Market. August 16. he has been waiting for this spot for a long time and now he actually has it and we need to be ready

That's exciting! Santa Fe Indian Market is one of the biggest and most prestigious shows for Native artists. Getting a booth there is a real accomplishment. What does he need to have ready, and what does the logistics side look like?

so he makes jewelry. silver and turquoise, traditional work. he has been pouring everything into this one concho belt for about 3 months now. it is his centerpiece, you know. the materials alone are $4,200 between the silver and the turquoise. so the stakes are high

Three months of work and $4,200 in materials -- that's a serious investment in one piece. A concho belt like that at Indian Market could command a strong price though, especially with that level of craftsmanship. What other pieces is he bringing, or is the booth built around the belt as the showpiece?

the belt is the anchor piece but he has rings, bracelets, a couple bolo ties, some earrings. probably 30 pieces total. the belt is what draws people to the booth though. once they stop to look at that they see everything else. that is the strategy anyway

That's a solid strategy -- one jaw-dropping centerpiece that stops people in their tracks, then the smaller accessible pieces are what most people actually buy. For 30 pieces plus a high-value belt, you'll want to think about display, security, and transport. Are you driving down?

yes driving down the day before. it is about 4 hours from here to Santa Fe. we will set up the booth the evening before if they let us or first thing in the morning. I do not want to rush the display, you know. presentation matters

Driving down the day before is smart -- it gives you a buffer in case anything comes up and avoids the stress of a same-day setup. For transporting $4,200 worth of materials in the belt alone plus 30 other pieces, have you thought about how you'll pack and secure everything for the drive?

that is what I am trying to figure out. last time he did a smaller show we just wrapped everything in towels. but this is different. the concho belt alone weighs a couple pounds and each concho is hand-stamped. if anything gets scratched that is 3 months of work damaged

For a piece like that, you'll want something better than towels. Individual soft pouches or jewelry rolls for the smaller pieces, and for the concho belt specifically, I'd suggest a padded hard case -- even a small pelican-style case with custom foam inserts. You could also use a flat display box lined with velvet that doubles as both transport protection and part of the booth display.

the display box idea is good. protect it on the road and then open it up at the booth like presenting something special. people like that, you know. makes it feel like an event when you reveal the piece. I will talk to Raymond about building something. he is good with his hands obviously

Exactly -- the unboxing becomes part of the presentation. A handcrafted display box made by the artist himself adds another layer of authenticity and craftsmanship to the whole experience. For the booth itself, do you know what size space you're working with and whether they provide tables, or do you need to bring everything?

we bring everything. table, chairs, shade cover. the market gives you a numbered spot and that is it. I have a checklist started but I know I am forgetting things. I was thinking a nice tablecloth, maybe something woven, good lighting if we can rig it, price tags that do not look cheap

Your instincts are good. A woven tablecloth adds warmth and cultural context. For lighting, a couple of small battery-powered LED spotlights aimed at the belt and key pieces can make the silver really pop, especially under a shade cover where natural light might be limited. For price tags, small folded cards with the piece name, materials, and price look professional. And don't forget: business cards, a guest book for collectors, and a way to accept card payments -- a lot of buyers at Indian Market don't carry cash for big purchases.

the card reader. yes. we need that. last time someone wanted to buy a bracelet and walked away because we only had cash. that will not happen again. ahéhee, this is helpful. I feel better having a plan instead of just worrying about it

You're welcome! Having a plan turns the anxiety into action. You've got the hard part covered -- Raymond's talent and three months of work on that belt. The logistics are just about making sure all that craftsmanship gets the presentation it deserves. Good luck in August.
